_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The design system places the document list on the left and the preview area on the right. The user wants the attached document on the right instead.

The left column will hold case details, checklist, identity, residence, and address. The attached document will move to the right.

The right column will feature a sticky document preview panel. The left side will contain the case intake form sections. The document list may remain on the left or move to the right panel header for switching documents. I will check existing MudBlazor split layouts and DesignSystem components. I also need to add a document content endpoint to serve files.

A new `RegistrationCaseDocumentViewer` component will render the document preview. The detail page will switch to a two-column `MudGrid` layout. The left column will span seven grid units and contain the main content. The right column will span five units and hold the sticky document panel. I am checking existing CSS patterns to keep the right panel fixed during scroll.

The document list will move to the right panel. A selector will let users choose between multiple documents. The panel will display PDFs in an iframe and images as thumbnails. It will include a download button and an empty state when no documents exist.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
GET support is being added to the Created URL endpoint to match REST conventions.

Scope is kept minimal. A download handler and endpoint will be added. A new panel component will display documents on the right. The detail page will switch to a split layout with CSS for sticky positioning.

The header will remain outside the grid spanning full width. The main content will sit in a seven-to-five column split. The right panel will use sticky positioning fixed below the app bar with a scrollable max-height.

PDFs will render in an iframe and images in an img tag. The download handler validates the case exists before retrieving the document.

The handler validates the document belongs to the requested case. It returns a stream from storage along with the file name and content type. FileStream is used since the endpoint owns and disposes the stream.

The endpoint returns a file result with range processing enabled. It returns a 404 for missing documents. Inline display in the iframe requires omitting the download filename parameter.

The endpoint will use `Results.Stream` with a null file download name. This ensures the browser treats the response as inline content rather than a forced attachment.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Implementing a two-pane layout: case details on the left, document list and preview on the right, plus a GET endpoint to serve document content for preview.

_**Agent (default)**_

The registration case detail page now uses a two-column layout: case intake on the left, documents on the right.

### Layout
- **Left (7/12):** Checklist, identity, residence, address, household, and civil status — unchanged functionally.
- **Right (5/12):** Sticky document panel with upload, document list, and inline preview.
- The page uses full width (`MaxWidth.False`) so both columns have room on wide screens. On smaller screens the document panel stacks below the case content.

### Document panel (`RegistrationCaseDocumentPanel`)
- Upload form at the top
- Clickable document list (selected item highlighted)
- **Preview** card below — PDFs in an iframe, images inline, other types show a “preview unavailable” message with a download link
- First attached document is selected automatically

### Backend
- Added `GET /api/registration/cases/{id}/documents/{documentId}` to stream document content for preview/download (matches the URL already returned when attaching a document).
- Extended `IDocumentStorage` with `OpenReadAsync` for reading stored files.

Restart the app if it was already running so the changes load. When a case has attached documents, you should see the preview on the right while scrolling through intake sections on the left.

---

_**User**_

I like the idea of the preview but I don't want it to be displayed if we didn't click on a document (so when we load the page) - We should have an indication that we can click on the document to open the preview

Also the Attach document box should be aligned at the same level as the Identity one (so the Completeness checklist must be an upper level) 

---

</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The Completeness checklist needs to move outside and above the MudGrid in RegistrationCaseDetail.razor. The Attach document box should align with the Identity section. The preview should only appear after a document is clicked.

Auto-selection of the first document on page load will be removed. A hint will prompt users to select a document before previewing. Selection will clear automatically if the currently selected document is removed. The cursor will indicate that clicking opens the preview.

Newly uploaded documents will not trigger the preview automatically. The right column’s DocumentUpload sits directly inside AppCard, while the left column’s Identity is wrapped in AppSection. This structural difference must be resolved to align the two sections.

The Completeness checklist will sit above the MudGrid. This aligns the Identity and Attach document cards at the same grid row level. The right column will place DocumentUpload first, followed by the attached documents list. A hint will appear in the attached documents section when no document is selected.

A subtle AppEmptyState placeholder may replace the preview area when no document is selected. List items will receive accessibility labels and a pointer cursor to signal they are clickable.
</details></think>

Two updates:

### Layout alignment
The **Completeness checklist** now sits full-width above the two-column grid. **Identity** (left) and **Attach document** (right) start on the same row.

### Preview behavior
- No document is selected on load — the preview panel stays hidden.
- When documents exist but none is selected, an info box says **“Click a document to open a preview.”**
- List items use a pointer cursor; the selected one is still highlighted.
- Preview appears only after you click a document.
- If the selected document is removed, the preview closes instead of auto-selecting another.

Refresh the page to see the changes.

---

_**User**_

it would be nice to have an expand button on the preview to open in big screen

---

_**Agent (default)**_

I'll add a fullscreen expand control to the preview and wire it through whatever dialog or overlay pattern the app already uses.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Investigating whether Blazor Server's iframe or image loading is interfering with API endpoint requests. The document fetch via Navigation.ToAbsoluteUri may be failing despite appearing to open the page.

Antiforgery middleware could be interfering with GET requests. The Aspire proxy might be routing requests to the wrong host or port. A 404 response could trigger status code re-execution, returning HTML instead of the file. The upload handler stores files in ContentRootPath/uploads.

The handler may throw exceptions that return HTML error pages instead of the file. The storage path used when reading documents could also be incorrect.

The iframe sandbox is not configured, so it should not block requests. PDF content-type headers appear correct. The next step is to test the API endpoint directly and review integration tests. Blazor Server circuit behavior is unlikely because iframe and image sources trigger full page navigation rather than internal Blazor requests.
</details></think>

Checking if Aspire multi-instance routing sends uploads to one container but reads from another. Also investigating whether Blazor antiforgery or endpoint filtering blocks iframe requests. StatusCodePagesWithReExecute interception seems unlikely.

Checking if the stream gets disposed before sending or if the handler's scoped lifetime causes issues. Verifying whether Navigation.ToAbsoluteUri produces the correct URL behind the Aspire proxy. Mixed content blocking seems unlikely in development.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Adding an integration test to verify the download endpoint returns content. Then investigating the Blazor preview issue.

The upload and preview handlers both read from the same disk path. The file should be available on the same instance. The root cause likely lies elsewhere.

The iframe may be receiving HTML from a 404 response instead of the file. The StatusCodePagesWithReExecute middleware could be re-executing the pipeline and returning a 200 with HTML. This would explain why the page opens large but the file never loads. A missing document record, a missing file on disk, or a case ID mismatch could trigger the 404. Since the uploaded files appear in the list, the database records exist, so the file may not have persisted to disk correctly.

Verified that AttachDocument saves files correctly. The iframe URL appears properly constructed with absolute URIs. Base href settings and dialog timing were considered unlikely causes.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Adding a quick integration test for the download endpoint. A passing test would point to a browser or Blazor-specific cause, such as iframe, CSP, or antiforgery issues.

Suspecting the antiforgery middleware blocks GET requests from the iframe. Without an antiforgery token, the request may return a 400 Bad Request with an empty body. This would explain why the page opens but the file never loads.

Checking whether antiforgery validation applies to all endpoints by default or only those mapped through Blazor. The documentation indicates it is enabled for all endpoints in apps calling MapRazorComponents, but minimal API GET requests may still require explicit `.DisableAntiforgery()` to bypass validation.

The antiforgery middleware likely does not block the download endpoint. Minimal APIs without explicit `[RequireAntiforgery]` metadata should pass through. The integration test will inspect the response content-type and raw bytes.

A 404 error would cause the middleware to return HTML from the not-found page instead of the PDF. The iframe would then display the Blazor application shell rather than the file contents. The file may not exist on disk when the preview request arrives. The upload path and the preview read path might also differ between the server-side handler and the Blazor circuit.

Confirmed that `ContentRootPath` points to the Web project directory during local development, not the build output folder. This means uploaded files remain in the expected location across rebuilds.

The user's report likely means the fullscreen preview dialog opens, but the actual file content never loads. Images would show a broken icon if the endpoint returns HTML instead of binary data. I will add `.DisableAntiforgery()` to the GET download endpoint to rule out antiforgery blocking. I am also considering a navigation-related Blazor issue.

`ToAbsoluteUri` might generate incorrect URLs during prerender versus interactive mode. Using HttpClient to fetch the file as a blob and display it via an object URL would sidestep iframe problems, but that approach is too complex. The simplest fix is a dedicated download endpoint that disables antiforgery, sets an explicit inline Content-Disposition header, and excludes the route from status-code-page re-execution.

Considering `.ExcludeFromDescription()` to prevent status-code-page re-execution from returning HTML on 404 errors. Also testing whether `Results.Stream` without a filename causes PDF iframe issues, and comparing it against `Results.File` with explicit inline disposition headers.

The concrete fix is to wrap `UseStatusCodePagesWithReExecute` in `UseWhen` so it only intercepts non-API routes. This prevents the middleware from replacing API responses with HTML on 404 errors.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The API endpoint works correctly in integration tests. The problem appears to be specific to the browser or Blazor environment.

Antiforgery, routing, and CSP are unlikely culprits. The focus is now on whether Navigation.ToAbsoluteUri returns an incorrect URL behind the Aspire proxy, or if the iframe loads before the correct URL is set.

The iframe may be receiving a Blazor HTML shell instead of the expected file content. The Aspire dashboard URL might differ from the web application base URI.

Antiforgery validation might behave differently in the browser versus the test server. Disabling antiforgery on the GET endpoint is being considered as a diagnostic step.

A more likely cause is that uploaded files are missing from disk. If the file exists in the database but not on the server, the GET request returns a 404 error page inside the iframe. This would explain why the preview appears blank even though the user sees the file listed.

A container rebuild may have wiped the local uploads folder while the PostgreSQL database persisted. The Blazor upload handler might also be writing to a different ContentRootPath than the GET endpoint reads from under Aspire hot reload.

Enhanced forms are not relevant here. A blob URL approach using HttpClient was considered, but in Blazor Server the iframe source is fetched client-side by the browser, not server-side.

Authentication is not required, so cookies or session issues are unlikely. An HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect could be breaking the iframe load. MudDialog overlays might also block the iframe from rendering.

Conditional rendering might prevent the iframe from reloading when the document changes. The user's report suggests both inline and fullscreen previews fail. A fake PDF in the integration test may not render in the browser even though the API returns 200.

Real PDFs and images were uploaded successfully, ruling out corrupted file data. Because Aspire assigns dynamic ports, the exact running URL needs verification. The current plan is to stop using iframe/src against the API and instead stream documents through a dedicated static file route or Blazor page.

The cleanest fix is to bypass the iframe entirely. The component will fetch the document stream server-side, convert it to a base64 data URL, and render it directly as an image or embedded object. This avoids browser fetch issues completely.

The implementation will stream the document into a memory buffer, encode it as a base64 data URL, and bind it to an image or iframe. Browsers typically support PDF data URLs inside iframes. Loading the full file into memory is acceptable for this demo.

The preview component will take CaseId and DocumentId instead of a URL. It will inject DownloadDocumentHandler to stream the file server-side and render it as a data URL. The download link and expand dialog will keep using the API endpoint.

The preview component will be wired into `RegistrationCaseDocumentPanel` and `DocumentPreviewDialog`. It will reload whenever the selected document changes. Loading and error states will be added. Streams will be disposed properly. Data URLs do not need revocation.

The preview component now accepts CaseId, DocumentId, and FileName as parameters. It tracks loading state, errors, and the loaded document ID to avoid redundant fetches. When the selected document changes, it calls the download handler and converts the returned stream into a base64 data URL.

The implementation copies the response stream into a memory buffer, encodes it as a base64 data URL, and stores it in the component state. The download button can either use a data URL or continue calling the API endpoint. The handler is already registered as scoped, which suits Blazor Server components.

Navigation will be removed from the preview component. The download button will trigger a JavaScript download from the data URL or use a separate handler call. The existing API download link will be replaced to avoid the same browser issues.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

The preview was pointing an `<iframe>` / `<img>` at the `/api/...` URL. In Blazor Server that browser request often fails silently (antiforgery, routing, or the not-found page being returned as HTML instead of the file), so the dialog opened but nothing loaded.

**What changed:** the preview now loads documents **on the server** through `DownloadDocumentHandler`, converts them to a data URL, and renders that in the iframe/image. That avoids the browser fetch issue entirely.

Also:
- A **loading spinner** while the file loads
- **Clear error messages** if the file is missing from storage (e.g. after a rebuild with a persisted DB)
- **Download** uses the same server-side loading path
- API routes are excluded from the HTML not-found fallback, and the download endpoint has antiforgery disabled

Restart the app, select a document, and the preview should load. If you still see an error like *“The file is missing from storage”*, re-upload the document — the database record may exist without the file on disk after a previous restart.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
A critical bug was found in `LoadPreviewAsync`. The `IsPreviewable` check appears to be blocking preview loading entirely.

The early return does not leave `_loading` stuck, so the failure is likely silent. The real problem may be Blazor Server's handling of large base64 data URLs sent through SignalR. Message size limits or browser restrictions on data: URLs inside iframes could be causing the failure.

The root cause is likely SignalR circuit message size limits. Large base64 strings embedded in iframe src attributes can exceed these limits and fail silently.

Two solutions are being considered. One uses JavaScript interop to create a blob URL from base64 data on the client side. This avoids putting huge data URLs in the Blazor render tree. The other loads bytes via JavaScript fetch to an API endpoint, though the API had browser issues previously.

A dedicated Blazor page that streams the file directly is another option. It would bypass SignalR entirely by having the browser navigate the iframe to a full page load.

Increasing SignalR message size is a poor workaround for large files.

The preferred path remains JavaScript interop. The server would pass raw bytes to the client, and JavaScript would create a blob URL from that byte array. This avoids embedding large data strings in HTML attributes.

The earlier API fetch failure may have been a configuration issue rather than a fundamental flaw. HttpClient works correctly in integration tests.

The user confirmed the data URL fix did not resolve the issue. This strongly suggests SignalR cannot deliver large src attributes to the iframe. The application may also need a restart to pick up recent changes. Another potential issue was noted: LoadPreviewAsync might not explicitly call StateHasChanged after its asynchronous load in OnParametersSetAsync, though Blazor typically handles re-renders automatically after await completes.

A concrete implementation plan is forming. A new JavaScript module would receive the base64 payload and content type, then construct a blob URL and assign it to the iframe source. The component would hold an ElementReference and use IJSRuntime to invoke this helper after loading bytes. Blob URLs would be revoked on disposal. The embedded page approach is still under consideration.

The existing minimal API endpoint is being investigated as the fallback. A direct curl test against the running Aspire application will reveal why the browser cannot reach it. The new upload failure confirms the data URL approach is completely broken.

Other failure modes are now being considered. The preview may hang indefinitely if `LoadPreviewAsync` never completes. Blazor prerendering could also cause `OnParametersSetAsync` to run twice, possibly without an active circuit during the initial render.

The component sits inside InteractiveServer pages. Blazor prerenders the component on the server, then reconnects interactively. If the server-side load succeeds, the preview data is lost when the interactive state resets. Child components inherit the parent's render mode, which complicates state persistence across prerender and interactive phases.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
A potential bug was spotted in `RegistrationCaseDocumentPanel` involving `AppCard` usage. The component appears to be used incorrectly, which may explain why no errors appear in the console or logs.

The explicit `ChildContent` wrapper is valid and matches the working Identity card pattern. This rules out `AppCard` usage as the cause of the silent failure.

A direct preview URL for the iframe is being considered more reliable than blob URLs via JS interop. The existing `/api` route was tested successfully in isolation, so the problem likely lies in how Aspire proxies requests to the web project.

The relative iframe URL should work from the same origin. A fallback route or Blazor enhanced navigation is unlikely to be blocking it. The focus is shifting to a timing issue: `DocumentPreviewContent` initializes with `_loading` set to false, so it briefly renders "Preview unavailable" before `OnParametersSetAsync` can generate the data URL. If that method never executes, the preview stays blank. The component is confirmed to be fully interactive within the same page.

The download function might also be failing. If both preview and download are broken, the handler itself is likely broken rather than a UI timing issue. The storage path under Aspire needs verification. The user did not mention download problems.

The web project will be launched directly on port 5155 to test end-to-end behavior. The planned fix uses JavaScript interop to convert byte arrays into blob URLs for the iframe. An absolute API URL via Navigation.ToAbsoluteUri will serve as a fallback. Any underlying API issues will also be addressed.

The JavaScript interop helper will create a blob URL and assign it to the iframe or image element. The C# side will store the element reference and invoke the helper during `OnAfterRenderAsync` once the DOM is ready. This defers the blob generation until after the preview element exists. A cleaner structure for `DocumentPreviewContent` is also being drafted.

The preview component now uses conditional rendering with an iframe for PDFs and an image tag for images. `OnParametersSetAsync` loads the raw bytes, while `OnAfterRenderAsync` invokes the JavaScript helper to assign the blob URL to the active element. A `_rendered` flag prevents duplicate assignments. The `_bytes` and `_rendered` state resets whenever the `DocumentId` changes.

Old blob URLs should be revoked when the preview element is disposed or the document parameters change.

The API endpoint failure is being traced to the `UseWhen` middleware configuration. The status-code-page branch is registered before the API routes, which may intercept the request before it reaches the document handler.

Antiforgery middleware might be rejecting GET requests to `/api` without a token in .NET 10. The user sees a blank preview card with no error message, suggesting the spinner is not stuck and the iframe is simply empty. `Results.Stream` may also need to become `Results.File` for browser compatibility.

Blazor Server’s default SignalR maximum receive message size is 32 KB. Large base64-encoded images or PDFs sent in the render batch are likely being silently truncated or rejected by the circuit hub. This explains the blank iframe despite the data URL appearing to be generated correctly.

JavaScript interop also hits the same SignalR message size limit, so passing large byte arrays through it is equally unsafe. The only reliable path is to serve the file directly via an HTTP URL inside the iframe. The next step is to test the API endpoint with curl and verify whether the relative iframe URL, antiforgery settings, or the endpoint response itself is blocking the browser.

A separate Razor Page or MVC endpoint is unnecessary because the API route already sits outside Blazor. HTTPS certificates are unlikely to be the culprit since the iframe is same-origin. Content-Security-Policy defaults from Blazor or static asset routing might be blocking the request. The CaseId parameter is confirmed to be a Guid. Integration tests and a curl run against the live web server will isolate the exact failure point.

The API endpoint is registered after `MapRazorComponents`, but ASP.NET Core endpoint routing prioritizes template specificity over registration order. Since the API and Razor paths differ, the API route should still match correctly.

I am checking how Blazor Web App registers its fallback endpoint. I will run the application and test the API with curl. I also need to verify the upload path under Aspire. I am investigating whether `UseAntiforgery()` rejects GET requests in .NET 10. Minimal APIs do not opt in by default. I will run a practical test. I am also drafting a dedicated document preview page.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Standalone web execution is difficult. I'm focusing on identifying the root causes.

SignalR cannot handle large base64 data URLs. Chrome also blocks iframe data: URLs for PDFs. The fix uses iframe src pointing to the API URL. A JS blob URL fallback will handle direct fetch.

The JavaScript fetch approach is most reliable for Blazor Server. It avoids SignalR entirely and lets the browser handle HTTP GET directly. Any fetch failure—such as antiforgery or 404 errors—will be caught and shown in the UI.

I am investigating why the fetch call fails. Antiforgery tokens may be required for GET requests in .NET 9 Blazor Web Apps, which would cause 400 errors. CORS is not a factor since the requests stay same-origin.

Minimal API endpoints without explicit antiforgery requirements should not block fetch requests. Same-origin fetch automatically includes cookies, so credentials are likely not the problem. I am checking whether the document save path differs between Aspire hot reload and direct download, and noting that Aspire uses PostgreSQL while standalone uses SQLite. I will now rewrite DocumentPreviewContent.

The preview component will render an empty iframe or image with a reference. After rendering, JavaScript will fetch the document from the API URL and create a blob URL. Errors will be returned to Blazor and displayed. Blob URLs will be revoked on disposal or parameter changes. A key attribute will force remounting when switching documents. The dialog will use the same approach. Base64 and data URL methods will be removed entirely. The API endpoint is confirmed working.

A new hypothesis emerged. If the API route is missing, Blazor's fallback might return a 200 status with an HTML page instead of a 404. Fetch would then retrieve that HTML blob, and the PDF viewer would render blank. This could explain the silent failure. I need to check whether MapRazorComponents registers a fallback that intercepts unmatched routes.

I suspect the API route mapping order is causing the mismatch. Since MapRegistrationEndpoints is registered after MapRazorComponents, the Blazor fallback may intercept requests to the API route and return the HTML shell with a 200 status. This would explain why integration tests pass while the browser fetch fails silently. I am checking whether this registration sequence creates the edge case.

I will move the API endpoint registration before MapRazorComponents in Program.cs. This ensures the specific route wins over the Blazor fallback. A dedicated non-API path is another option to avoid conflicts.

I am now implementing the JavaScript fetch logic. A WeakMap will track each preview element's blob URL so it can be revoked before fetching a new one. The function returns structured errors when the element is missing or the fetch fails, and it sends same-origin credentials.

The JavaScript helper now returns structured errors for failed fetches, empty files, and loading failures. A separate revokePreview function cleans up blob URLs when elements are disposed. I am now writing the corresponding C# backend code.

The C# preview component now builds the absolute API URL inside `OnParametersSet`. It sets a `_needsLoad` flag and clears any prior error. During `OnAfterRenderAsync`, it checks if the file is previewable, marks loading active, and invokes the JavaScript fetch helper via `Js.InvokeAsync`.

The C# preview component now handles the JavaScript result. It clears the loading state, stores any error, and refreshes the UI. A `DisposeAsync` method will invoke the JavaScript cleanup helper to revoke the blob URL when the component is destroyed. The preview element will be an iframe for PDFs and an image for other formats. The download endpoint already disables antiforgery. I considered adding logging to the handler for debugging, but the user noted there are no logs to review.
</details></think>

PreviewLoadResult JSON deserialization may be failing. JavaScript returns `ok: true`, but the C# property is named `Ok`. This mismatch could explain why nothing happens without errors.

The component remounts correctly when DocumentId changes via @key. To prevent JSON property name mismatches, PreviewLoadResult should explicitly use [JsonPropertyName("ok")] on its properties.

_**Agent (default)**_

Two bugs were causing the silent failure:

### Root causes
1. **Preview element never mounted during load** — The loading spinner replaced the `<iframe>` / `<img>`, so JavaScript tried to set `src` on an element that wasn’t in the DOM. No error was thrown; the preview stayed blank.
2. **Base64 data URLs over SignalR** — The earlier approach tried to push the whole file through Blazor’s circuit, which often fails silently for anything beyond a tiny file.

### Fix
- The preview element is **always rendered** (hidden while loading).
- JavaScript **fetches the file from the API** and creates a **blob URL** — the file never goes through SignalR.
- If loading fails, you’ll see an explicit error (e.g. *“Document request failed (404)”* or *“Document could not be loaded from the server”*).
- API routes are registered **before** Blazor so `/api/...` isn’t swallowed by the app shell.

Restart the app (`Ctrl+C`, then `dotnet run --project src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.AppHost`), upload a document, click it to preview, and you should see a loading spinner followed by the file. If it still fails, the red error box should say why — share that message and we can dig further.
